1:
"Then was Jesus led up of the Spirit into the wilderness to be tempted of the devil." The witnesses read the great combat — the Spirit-led Christ entering the lists with Satan.

The Latin and Eastern fathers fix the purpose and the agency. Augustine: "why did he offer himself to temptation? That he might be our mediator in vanquishing temptation not by aid only, but by example." Jerome: "led, not against his will or as a prisoner, but as by a desire for the conflict." Gregory settles which Spirit: "true and without question" the Holy Spirit. Chrysostom comforts the tempted: "be not troubled... for this thou receivedst arms, to fight, not to sit idle."

The Reformed and Puritan voices read the cosmic duel. Calvin gives "two reasons" — that after the fast Christ "might come forth as a new man... to the discharge of his office," and "that he might be tried by temptation and undergo an apprenticeship." Henry: "a famous duel... between Michael and the dragon, the Seed of the woman and the seed of the serpent." Lightfoot ties it to Genesis 3:15: the war "proclaimed of old in Eden" now takes place.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the setting. Robertson notes the definite "then" and "into the wilderness," the same region as John's preaching. Wesley draws the lesson: "after the clearest light and the strongest consolation, let us expect the sharpest temptations." So the witnesses agree: immediately after the Father's testimony (Matthew 3:17), the Spirit leads Christ — willingly, as a champion to combat — into the wilderness to be tried by the devil, the second Adam entering the conflict the first Adam lost (v.3).

So the witnesses read the temptation as the great combat: immediately after the Father's testimony, the Spirit leads Christ willingly, as a champion to the lists, against the devil. Augustine's that he vanquishes by example, and Henry's and Lightfoot's that this is the duel of the Seed of the woman and the serpent (Gen 3:15), together set the second Adam in the conflict the first lost (v.3).
2:
"And when he had fasted forty days and forty nights, he was afterward an hungred." The witnesses read the great fast and its deliberate parallels.

The grammatical-historical and Reformed readers fix the model. Gill, Clarke, Ellicott, and Wesley note the conscious parallel with Moses (Exodus 34:28) and Elijah (1 Kings 19:8) — "the great lawgiver" and "the chief of the prophets" each fasting forty days, and now "the giver of the New Covenant." Barnes settles that he "ate nothing" (Luke 4:2). Robertson: "no perfunctory ceremonial fast, but of communion with the Father in complete abstention from food," the period "spent in a spiritual ecstasy."

The hunger is read as the opening for the first assault. Wesley: "he was afterward hungry — and so prepared for the first temptation." Gill: he fasted "when he was about to publish the Gospel of his grace, and bear witness to the truth."

So the witnesses agree: Christ's forty-day fast deliberately recalls Moses and Elijah, marking him as the inaugurator of the new covenant, and was a time of high communion with the Father rather than mere abstinence. Its end in hunger — the return of true human need — is the very point at which Satan strikes, tempting the famished Son to use his power for himself (v.3). The fast both consecrates Christ for his ministry and sets the stage for the conflict.

So the witnesses make the forty-day fast both a consecration and a stage: deliberately recalling Moses and Elijah, a time of high communion ending in the hunger at which Satan strikes. The moderns' parallels (Ex 34:28; 1 Kgs 19:8) and Wesley's that the hunger prepared him for the first temptation together frame the inauguration of the new covenant (v.3).

The fast both consecrates Christ for his ministry and sets the stage for the conflict, its end in hunger the very point at which Satan strikes (v.3).

The return of true human need is the very door by which the tempter enters.

So the great fast inaugurates the new covenant as Moses' and Elijah's fasts had marked the old (Ex 34:28).
3:
"And when the tempter came to him, he said, If thou be the Son of God, command that these stones be made bread." The witnesses read the first temptation — an assault through appetite and a challenge to the voice at the Jordan.

The Latin fathers read the strategy. Gregory: "the old enemy tempted the first man" through gluttony, and "by which he had once been the victor" he now attacks Christ (Ambrose). Jerome catches him in a dilemma: "if these stones can be made bread, your temptation is vain against one so mighty; if he cannot, your suspicion of the Son of God is groundless." Leo: Christ "opposed the adversary rather by testimonies out of the Law than by miraculous powers." Rabanus: "whoso feeds not on the Word of God, he lives not."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the challenge. Robertson: "if thou art Son of God" (no article) — "the devil is alluding to the words of the Father at the baptism... he challenges this address." Barnes: "here is a fit opportunity to try thy power, and show that thou art truly his Son." Clarke notes hunger "naturally diminishes the strength of the body," so the mind is more easily assailed.

The Reformed voice names the aim. Calvin: "Satan made a direct attack on the faith of Christ, in the hope that... he would drive Christ to unlawful and wicked methods of procuring food." Wesley: the tempter came "in a visible form, probably in a human shape." So the witnesses agree: Satan strikes at the hungering Christ through the most basic appetite, baiting the title "Son of God" to tempt him to distrust the Father and use his power for self-relief — the very point Christ will meet not with a miracle but with the word of God (v.4).

So the witnesses read the first assault as an attack on faith through appetite, baiting the title 'Son of God.' The fathers' that the old enemy conquered the first man by gluttony, and Calvin's that Satan sought to drive Christ to unlawful means of food, together show the tempter striking at the hungering Son to make him distrust the Father (v.4).
4:
"But he answered and said, It is written, Man shall not live by bread alone, but by every word that proceedeth out of the mouth of God." The witnesses read Christ's answer — Scripture met against the tempter.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation. Barnes and Ellicott: the words are from Deuteronomy 8:3, spoken of the manna, and (with the next two answers) drawn "from two chapters of Deuteronomy," one of which (Deut 6) supplied the phylactery passages — "a prominence was thus given" to words familiar to devout Jews. Clarke: God "purposes the welfare of his creatures; all his appointments are calculated to promote" it.

The Reformed voice reads the faith. Calvin: "men live... by the secret blessing of God"; Satan attacked Christ's faith, but Christ answers that he need not work a miracle, trusting that God who fed Israel with manna can sustain him.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley draws the pattern: "thus Christ answered, and thus we may answer all the suggestions of the devil" — man lives "by whatever God commands to sustain him," so "it is not needful I should work a miracle to procure bread, without any intimation" of the Father's will. So the witnesses agree: Christ repels the first temptation not by power but by the written word, confessing that life depends on God's sustaining will and not on bread alone — refusing to distrust the Father who had just owned him as Son (Matthew 3:17), and leaving the church the pattern of answering Satan from Scripture (v.7).

So the witnesses read Christ's answer as Scripture met against the tempter: life depends on God's sustaining will, not on bread alone. The moderns' that the three answers all come from Deuteronomy, and Wesley's that thus we answer all Satan's suggestions, together leave the church the pattern of repelling the enemy from the written word (v.7).

Christ refuses to distrust the Father who had just owned him as Son, leaving the church the pattern of answering Satan from Scripture (v.7).

Life depends on the sustaining will of God, and not on bread alone.
5:
"Then the devil taketh him up into the holy city, and setteth him on a pinnacle of the temple." The witnesses read the second temptation's setting — and the devil's reach even into the sacred place.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the symbolism. Remigius: "the Devil lies in wait for Christ's faithful people even in the sacred places." Ambrose: "as Satan transfigures himself into an angel of light, and spreads a snare even from the divine Scriptures, so now he uses its texts." Chrysostom: Scripture is brought "by the Lord only with an apt meaning, but by the Devil irreverently." Gloss: "the Devil places us on high places by exalting with pride, that he may dash us to the ground."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the place and the order. Robertson notes Matthew's vivid "taketh him" (historic present) and that Luke puts this temptation third — "the geographical order." Lightfoot and Clarke discuss the "pinnacle" — perhaps "the king's gallery" of the temple, of dizzying height over the valley. Ellicott: the difference of order between Matthew and Luke is "instructive."

The Reformed voice harmonizes the sequence. Calvin: "it was not the intention of the Evangelists to arrange the history" so as "to preserve the exact order of time." So the witnesses agree: Satan transports Christ to the temple's pinnacle in the holy city — the tempter following him even into the sacred place — and prepares to misuse Scripture itself, perverting a promise of protection into a dare. The setting itself warns that no place is safe from temptation, and that the devil can cite Scripture for his purpose (v.6).

So the witnesses read the second setting as a warning that no place is safe: Satan follows Christ even to the temple's pinnacle and prepares to misuse Scripture itself. The fathers' that he transfigures as an angel of light and cites the word irreverently, and the moderns' on the differing order in Luke, together unveil the subtlety of the assault (v.6).

The setting itself warns that no place is safe from temptation, and that the devil can cite Scripture for his purpose (v.6).
6:
"And saith unto him, If thou be the Son of God, cast thyself down: for it is written, He shall give his angels charge concerning thee." The witnesses read the second temptation — to presumption, baited with a perverted text.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the device. Robertson: "the appeal to hurl himself down into the abyss... would intensify the nervous dread"; "the devil urged presumptuous reliance on God and quotes Scripture" (Psalm 91:11-12). Clarke: "the very means we make use of to repel one temptation may be used by him" for another — Christ had answered the first by trust in God, so Satan now solicits him "to make trial of it." Ellicott: "the subtlety of the Tempter lay in his perversion of their true meaning."

The Reformed voice exposes the malice. Calvin: "we must observe this malice of Satan, in misapplying a quotation of Scripture, for the purpose of rendering life deadly to Christ, and of converting bread into poison" — "the same kind of stratagem he continues daily to employ."

So the witnesses agree: the second assault is to spiritual presumption — to fling himself down on the strength of a promise wrenched from its meaning, demanding a sign of the Father's care rather than trusting it. Satan, who failed through appetite, now quotes Scripture itself (Psalm 91), omitting its true sense ("to keep thee in all thy ways"), so that the very word of protection becomes a snare — a malice Christ will meet with another, rightly-applied text (v.7).

So the witnesses read the second temptation as a bid to presumption, baited with a wrenched promise (Ps 91). Clarke's that the means used against one temptation are turned to another, and Calvin's exposure of the malice that makes Scripture deadly, together show the word of protection perverted into a snare (v.7).

The very word of protection becomes a snare, a malice Christ will meet with another, rightly-applied text (v.7).

The promise of angelic care is wrenched from its true sense of keeping the believer in his appointed ways.
7:
"Jesus said unto him, It is written again, Thou shalt not tempt the Lord thy God." The witnesses read Christ's reply — Scripture corrected by Scripture.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the answer. Robertson: "Jesus quotes Deuteronomy again (Deuteronomy 6:16) and shows that the devil has wholly misapplied God's promise of protection." Gill: Christ "takes no notice of the false and wrong citation... but mildly replies, by opposing another passage of scripture." Clarke: to "expose myself to any danger naturally destructive, with the vain presumption that God will protect me... is to tempt God." Ellicott notes the words are from "the chapter that contains one of the passages written on the phylacteries."

The Reformed voice reads the principle. Calvin: "there is no other way in which we have a right to expect the assistance promised... than when believers humbly submit themselves to his guidance; for we cannot rely on his promises without obeying his commandments." Wesley: not to tempt God "by requiring further evidence of what he hath already made sufficiently plain."

So the witnesses agree: Christ answers the perverted promise with a balancing command — "thou shalt not tempt the Lord" — teaching that God's promises of protection belong to the path of obedience, not to presumptuous self-exposure. To demand a miraculous proof of the Father's care, already given at the Jordan, would be to tempt God; and Scripture rightly used overthrows Scripture wrongly cited (v.6).

So the witnesses read Christ's reply as Scripture correcting Scripture: God's promises of protection belong to the path of obedience, not to presumptuous self-exposure. The moderns' that he answers the misapplied text with a true one, and Calvin's that we cannot claim his promises while disobeying, together overthrow the tempter's misuse (v.8).

To demand a miraculous proof of the Father's care, already given at the Jordan, would be to tempt God (Matthew 3:17).

Scripture rightly used overthrows Scripture wrongly cited, the balancing command answering the perverted promise (Deut 6:16).

The promises of God belong to the way of obedience, not to the path of presumptuous self-exposure.
8:
"Again, the devil taketh him up into an exceeding high mountain, and sheweth him all the kingdoms of the world, and the glory of them." The witnesses read the third temptation's vast and unreal panorama.

The Eastern and Latin fathers read its nature. Origen: "we are not to suppose that... he presented before him the kingdom of Persia or India, but he shewed him" the world's glory mentally. Remigius: "by their glory is meant their gold and silver, precious stones and temporal goods." Jerome: "an arrogant and vain vaunt, for he hath not the power to bestow all kingdoms." Gregory: "in these things is shewn the twofold nature in one person — it is the man whom the Devil tempts; the same is God to whom Angels minister" (v.11).

The grammatical-historical readers fix the vision. Robertson: "this wonderful panorama had to be partially mental and imaginative"; Ellicott: "no specular mount in the whole earth commands a survey of all the kingdoms" — "all that passed in the Temptation was in the region of the spirit." Lightfoot reads it of "Rome with her empire," "all the world."

The Reformed voice reads the permission. Calvin: "it was not owing to any weakness of Christ's nature, but to a voluntary dispensation and permission, that Satan produced this effect upon his eyes." So the witnesses agree: in a visionary, not bodily, display Satan parades before Christ all the kingdoms and glory of the world — overreaching in his vaunt, since he has no right to bestow them — tempting the Son to gain by worship of the devil what the Father had promised through the cross (v.9).

So the witnesses read the third temptation's panorama as visionary, not bodily: Satan parades the world's glory, overreaching in a vaunt he has no right to make. The fathers' that the man is tempted while the same is God whom angels serve, and the moderns' that all passed in the region of the spirit, together frame the bid to ambition (v.9).

Satan tempts the Son to gain by worship of the devil what the Father had promised through the cross (v.9).
9:
"And saith unto him, All these things will I give thee, if thou wilt fall down and worship me." The witnesses read the naked demand at the heart of the third temptation — idolatry for the world.

The grammatical-historical readers weigh Satan's claim. Robertson: "the devil claims the rule of the world, not merely of Palestine" — "the kingdoms of the cosmos were under his sway." Barnes: "Satan claimed a right to bestow" these dominions "with considerable justice," for they were "excessively wicked," and so "with no small degree of plausibility... he asserted his claim." Ellicott cites Luke's addition ("that is delivered unto me," Luke 4:6): the offer "rested on the apparent evidence of the world's history."

The Eastern Origen reflects on the devil's usurped "power of death"; Clarke paraphrases the bargain: "the whole of this land is now under my government; do me homage, and I will deliver it into thy hand."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley reads the unmasking: "here Satan clearly shows who he was. Accordingly Christ... calls him by his own name, which he had not done before." So the witnesses agree: the third temptation drops all disguise — Satan offers the whole world, over which he holds a real if usurped sway, in exchange for an act of worship. It is the bid to win the kingdoms by idolatry rather than by the appointed way of suffering — the proposal Christ will reject with a flat command and a third citation of the Law (v.10).

So the witnesses read the naked demand at the heart of the third assault: the whole world for an act of worship. The moderns' that Satan claims a real if usurped sway (Lk 4:6), and Wesley's that here he unmasks himself, together expose the bid to win the kingdoms by idolatry rather than by the appointed cross (v.10).

It is the bid to win the kingdoms by idolatry rather than by the appointed way of suffering (v.10).

The whole world is offered for a single act of false worship.

So the third assault drops all disguise and bids openly for the worship due to God alone.
10:
"Then saith Jesus unto him, Get thee hence, Satan: for it is written, Thou shalt worship the Lord thy God, and him only shalt thou serve." The witnesses read Christ's decisive rebuke that ends the combat.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the force. Robertson: "Begone, Christ says to Satan" — "this temptation is the limit of diabolical suggestion." Barnes: "this was a bolder attack than any... others had been but an address to his necessities," but this Christ "met with a decided rebuke." Clarke: "this temptation savoring of nothing but diabolical impudence, Jesus... with divine authority commanded the tempter to return." Ellicott: the answer came "from the words of the Tephillim and the lessons of childhood" (Deuteronomy 6:13).

The Reformed voice distinguishes this from the word to Peter. Calvin: there is "no use for speculating" that the same words were addressed to Peter (Matthew 16:23); Christ "simply bids him go away." Wesley: "Get thee hence, Satan — not, get thee behind me" as to Peter, "but into thy proper place."

So the witnesses agree: at the demand for worship Christ casts off all parley — "Get thee hence, Satan" — and answers the third temptation, as the first two, with the written word: God alone is to be worshipped and served. The bid for idolatry is met with the first commandment, and the tempter, exposed and overthrown, is driven from the field (v.11).

So the witnesses read the decisive rebuke that ends the combat: 'Get thee hence, Satan,' answered, like the first two, from the Law — God alone is to be worshipped. The moderns' that this is the limit of diabolical suggestion, and Calvin's that it differs from the word to Peter, together drive the exposed tempter from the field (v.11).

The bid for idolatry is met with the first commandment, and the tempter, exposed and overthrown, is driven from the field (v.11).

God alone is to be worshipped and served, and the tempter is dismissed to his own place.

So the conflict ends as it began, with the written word of God set against the tempter.
11:
"Then the devil leaveth him, and, behold, angels came and ministered unto him." The witnesses read the close of the temptation — the enemy routed and the victor served by angels.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scene and the qualification. Barnes: "the devil left him for a time" (Luke 4:13), intending "to return again." Robertson notes the swift movement, the historic present "leaveth," and the angels coming (aorist) and ministering ("continued or repeated ministrations"). Clarke: they "brought that food which was necessary to support nature."

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the two natures and the ongoing warfare. Gregory: "the man whom the Devil tempts; the same is God to whom Angels minister." Hilary: "when we have overcome the Devil and bruised his head," the angels' ministry "will not be wanting in us."

The Reformed voice reads the respite. Calvin: "no truce or relaxation was granted to Christ, till he had been fully tried by every species of contest," and the angels' ministry shows the rest of his life "was not entirely free" from conflict, but that God "restrained the power of Satan." Wesley: the angels came "both to supply him with food, and to congratulate his victory." So the witnesses agree: the devil, beaten, withdraws — only for a season — and angels minister to the famished victor, attesting both his true humanity (which needed food) and his divine dignity (which angels serve), as the second Adam triumphs where the first had fallen (v.1).

So the witnesses read the close of the trial: the enemy routed, only for a season, and the victor served by angels. Gregory's that the man tempted is the same God whom angels minister to, and Calvin's that God restrained Satan's power, together attest both Christ's true humanity and his divine dignity as the second Adam triumphs (v.1).

The angels attest both his true humanity, which needed food, and his divine dignity, which angels serve, as the second Adam triumphs (v.1).

The victory of the second Adam reverses the defeat of the first, and angels serve the famished conqueror.
12:
"Now when Jesus had heard that John was cast into prison, he departed into Galilee." The witnesses read the transition to the Galilean ministry — and the great gap the Synoptics pass over.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the chronology. Robertson and Ellicott: between v.11 and this verse falls "a whole year" known only from John — the call of the first disciples (John 1:29-51), Cana, the cleansing of the temple, Nicodemus, the Samaritan woman. The Synoptics "skip from the temptation... to the Galilean ministry." Wesley lists the interval's events (John 2:13; 3:22).

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the prudence. Chrysostom: "it is not blameworthy not to throw one's self into peril, but when one has fallen into it, not to endure manfully" — he withdrew "to soften Jewish" hostility. John Chrysostom: "instructing us not to go to meet temptations, but to give place and withdraw." Pseudo-Chrysostom: none can harm a holy man "unless God deliver him up."

The Reformed voice harmonizes with John. Calvin: the three Evangelists "pass over in silence that short space of time, because John's course" still ran alongside Christ's. So the witnesses agree: on the imprisonment of his forerunner, Jesus withdraws to Galilee — Matthew leaping over the year of Judean ministry the Fourth Gospel records — teaching by his prudent retreat that we are not to court danger, and opening the great Galilean ministry foretold by the prophet (v.14).

So the witnesses read the transition to Galilee over the great gap the Synoptics skip — a year of Judean ministry known from John alone. Chrysostom's that we are not to court peril but withdraw, and the moderns' reconstruction of the interval, together open the Galilean ministry foretold by the prophet (v.14).

By his prudent retreat Christ teaches that we are not to court danger, opening the great Galilean ministry foretold by the prophet (v.14).

Matthew leaps over the year of Judean ministry the Fourth Gospel records, that he may open the Galilean work.

The withdrawal teaches that we are not to court peril but to give place in season.
13:
"And leaving Nazareth, he came and dwelt in Capernaum, which is upon the sea coast, in the borders of Zabulon and Nephthalim." The witnesses read the move that makes Capernaum the base of the ministry.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the place and the reason. Barnes and Ellicott: "leaving Nazareth, because his townsmen cast him out" (Luke 4:14-30); Capernaum, "a city on the north-west corner of the sea of Tiberias," "not mentioned in the Old Testament," became his "own city." Robertson: in Capernaum (probably Tell Hum) Jesus was "in a large town, one of the centres of Galilean political and commercial life, a fishing mart." Lightfoot laments Nazareth: "thou perishest by thine own folly."

The Reformed voice reads the providence. Calvin: as Christ "had hitherto been wont to frequent the town of Nazareth, so, in order to avoid danger, he now bade a final" farewell to it. Clarke: "utterly forsook Nazareth, because they had wholly rejected his word."

So the witnesses agree: rejected at Nazareth, his boyhood home, Christ settles at Capernaum on the lake — a populous, mixed town well placed for the gospel's spread — and there establishes the centre of his Galilean work. The move is recorded by Matthew not merely as fact but as the fulfilment of prophecy, the light dawning where Isaiah had foretold (v.14).

So the witnesses read the move to Capernaum as providence: rejected at Nazareth, Christ makes a populous lakeside town the centre of his work. The moderns' on Capernaum's commercial prominence and Calvin's that he bade Nazareth a final farewell together prepare the fulfilment of prophecy that Matthew at once records (v.14).

There he establishes the centre of his Galilean work, the move recorded not merely as fact but as the fulfilment of prophecy (v.14).

Rejected at his boyhood home, Christ makes the populous lakeside town the base of his ministry (Lk 4:29).

The despised town becomes, in God's providence, the chosen seat of the gospel's first proclamation.

So the rejected Christ makes a Gentile-bordering town the hub of his Galilean preaching and healing.
14:
"That it might be fulfilled which was spoken by Esaias the prophet." A brief verse, and the witnesses read Matthew's characteristic note that the move to Capernaum fulfilled prophecy.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the citation. Barnes: the place is Isaiah 9:1-2; "Matthew has given the sense, but not the very words of the prophet." Ellicott: "the light in which the fact of the migration presented itself to St. Matthew was... that it agreed with what had been spoken by a prophet" — Nazareth had fulfilled one prediction (Matthew 2:23), Capernaum another. Gill: Christ's dwelling there "accomplished a prophecy."

The verse is short because its work is to introduce the quotation that follows (v.15–16). It is one of Matthew's many "that it might be fulfilled" formulas, by which he shows the Galilean ministry, like every stage of Christ's life, unfolding according to the prophetic word.

So the witnesses agree: Matthew interprets the settlement at Capernaum theologically — not a mere change of residence but the fulfilment of Isaiah's ancient promise of light to the despised northern region. The Evangelist's habit of such notes binds the new gospel to the old Scripture, so that the reader sees in Christ's removal to Galilee of the Gentiles the dawning of the long-foretold light (v.16).

So the witnesses read Matthew's interpreting note: the settlement at Capernaum was no mere change of residence but the fulfilment of Isaiah's promise of light to the despised north. The Evangelist's habit of such 'that it might be fulfilled' formulas binds the new gospel to the old Scripture, so the reader sees prophecy unfolding in Christ's every step (v.16).

The Evangelist interprets the settlement at Capernaum theologically — the dawning of the long-foretold light in Galilee of the Gentiles (v.16).

The Evangelist's habit of such notes binds the new gospel inseparably to the old Scripture.

So Christ's every step is shown to unfold according to the prophetic word.

The fulfilment formula marks the Galilean ministry as the appointed unfolding of prophecy.

Prophecy and history thus meet in the move to Capernaum.
15:
"The land of Zabulon, and the land of Nephthalim... Galilee of the Gentiles." The witnesses read Isaiah's geography of the region now to be lit by the Messiah.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the sense. Lightfoot sets the quotation in its Isaianic context — the prophet "discoursing of the straits and miseries that compassed the transgressors of the law," then promising light. Barnes: "by the way of the sea" means "near to the sea"; "beyond Jordan" does not mean east of Jordan as the phrase might suggest. Clarke and Wesley explain "Galilee of the Gentiles" — so called "because it was inhabited by" Egyptians, Arabians, and Phoenicians (Strabo), the region "in a great measure inhabited by heathens."

The naming of the northern tribal lands — Zebulun and Naphtali — marks the very region first carried into Assyrian captivity, now first to receive the Messiah's light. The despised, half-heathen frontier becomes the cradle of the gospel.

So the witnesses agree: Isaiah's prophecy names the obscure northern territory — "Galilee of the Gentiles," mixed in population and remote from Jerusalem — as the place where the great light would shine. Matthew applies it to Christ's settling there, so that the region long under darkness and foreign admixture becomes the first to behold the dawning of the Messiah's ministry (v.16).

So the witnesses read Isaiah's geography: the obscure northern frontier — Zebulun, Naphtali, 'Galilee of the Gentiles,' mixed and half-heathen — named as the place where the great light would shine. The despised region first carried into captivity becomes the first to receive the Messiah's dawning (v.16).

The despised, half-heathen frontier becomes the cradle of the gospel, the first to behold the Messiah's dawning (v.16).

The region long under darkness and foreign admixture becomes the first to behold the dawning of the Messiah's ministry, as Isaiah had foretold (Isa 9:1-2).

The mixed and remote frontier is named as the very cradle of the Messiah's light.

So the obscure north, first into captivity, is first to behold the promised light.

The light of the Messiah dawns first on the despised frontier of the nation.
16:
"The people which sat in darkness saw great light; and to them which sat in the region and shadow of death light is sprung up." The witnesses read the fulfilment — the Messiah as the light dawning on a benighted region.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the image. Robertson: Matthew applies Isaiah's words "to the Messiah" — "the same district lay in spiritual darkness and death, and the new era dawned when Christ went thither." Barnes: "sitting in darkness" denotes "great ignorance," as those in night "know not where to go"; the instruction that removes it "is called light." Clarke notes Matthew changed Isaiah's "walked" to "sat," to "point out the increased misery of the state."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley marks "a beautiful gradation": "first they walked, then they sat in darkness, and lastly in the region of the shadow of death." Gill identifies the light as "Christ himself, who came a light into the world."

So the witnesses agree: in Christ's coming to Galilee, Isaiah's promise is fulfilled — the people sitting in the deepest darkness and the very shadow of death behold a great light. The despised northern frontier, sunk in ignorance and spiritual death, is the first to see the Messiah's dawning; and Matthew thus frames the start of the Galilean ministry as light breaking on the darkness, before recording its first word — the call to repentance (v.17).

So the witnesses read the fulfilment: the people sunk in the deepest darkness and the very shadow of death behold a great light in Christ's coming. Wesley's gradation of the misery and Gill's that the light is Christ himself together frame the start of the Galilean ministry as light breaking on the dark, before its first word is recorded (v.17).

Matthew frames the start of the Galilean ministry as light breaking on the darkness, before recording its first word (v.17).

The deepest darkness and the very shadow of death are the first to see the light of Christ.

So Matthew frames the Galilean ministry as the dawning of light upon a people in the shadow of death.
17:
"From that time Jesus began to preach, and to say, Repent: for the kingdom of heaven is at hand." The witnesses read the opening of Christ's own preaching — taking up the very message of his forerunner.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the start. Robertson: "began Jesus to preach" in Galilee, "his message carries on the words of the Baptist about repentance and the kingdom of heaven" (Matthew 3:2). Ellicott: "the commencement of our Lord's Galilean ministry," preceded by "a ministry of some months in Judaea" outwardly like the Baptist's. Wesley: "he had preached before... but from this time begins his solemn stated preaching."

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the timing and the message. Chrysostom: "he did not preach till John was in prison, that the multitude might not be split into two parties." Pseudo-Chrysostom: the gospel "should be preached by him who can control his appetites" — Christ, fresh from the victory over temptation, now preaches. Remigius: "he does not say the kingdom of the Canaanite... but the kingdom of heaven," for "the law promised worldly goods, but the Lord" heaven.

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke: "every preacher commissioned by God to proclaim salvation begins his work with preaching the doctrine of repentance." So the witnesses agree: having conquered the tempter, Christ opens his Galilean ministry with the same cry as John — repent, for the kingdom of heaven is at hand — joining his preaching to his forerunner's and summoning the people of the newly-lit region to the kingdom now drawing near (v.18).

So the witnesses read the opening of Christ's own preaching: fresh from the victory over temptation, he takes up the very cry of his forerunner — repent, for the kingdom is at hand. The fathers' that he waited till John's imprisonment lest the crowd be split, and the moderns' that this continues John's message, together join the two ministries (v.18).

Christ joins his preaching to his forerunner's, summoning the people of the newly-lit region to the kingdom now drawing near (v.18).

The conqueror of the tempter now calls the newly-lit region to repentance.
18:
"And Jesus, walking by the sea of Galilee, saw two brethren, Simon called Peter, and Andrew his brother, casting a net into the sea: for they were fishers." The witnesses read the calling of the first disciples.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the choice and the response. Augustine: "he chose not kings, senators, philosophers, or orators, but common, poor, and untaught fishermen," lest the gospel's success be ascribed to learning. Chrysostom: "he calls them while actually working at their employment, to shew that to follow him ought to be preferred to all occupations." Gregory: "Peter and Andrew had seen Christ work no miracle... yet at this single bidding" they followed. Remigius: he "saw" them "spiritually viewing their hearts."

The grammatical-historical readers fix the harmony. Robertson and Ellicott insist on remembering John's record — these were "not unprepared," having already become disciples (John 1:35-42); "here everything seems sudden," but they had met Christ before. Robertson notes the casting-net (amphiblēstron) thrown over the shoulder. Lightfoot adds rabbinic colour on fishing the lake.

The Puritan Henry: "when Christ began to preach, he began to gather disciples, who should now be the hearers, and hereafter the preachers, of his doctrine." So the witnesses agree: Christ, opening his ministry, calls humble fishermen — already drawn to him in Judaea, now summoned to leave their trade and follow — choosing the unlearned to confound the wise, that the power might be seen to be God's (1 Cor 1:27; v.19).

So the witnesses read the calling of humble fishermen: Christ chooses the unlearned to confound the wise, that the power might be seen to be God's. Augustine's that he chose not philosophers but fishermen, and the moderns' reminder that these were already disciples (John 1:35-42), together show the deliberate gathering of the apostolic band (v.19).

The Lord builds his apostolic band from working fishermen, choosing the unlearned to confound the wise (1 Cor 1:27).

The power of the gospel would thus be seen to rest in God and not in human learning.

The Lord gathers the future preachers of his kingdom from the boats of Galilee (Mk 1:17).
19:
"And he saith unto them, Follow me, and I will make you fishers of men." The witnesses read the call and its gracious promise.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers fix the word. Barnes: "fishers of men" are "ministers or preachers of the gospel, whose business it shall be to win souls to Christ" (Luke 5:10). Clarke: "come after me, receive my doctrines, imitate me" — "most of the calls of God to man are expressed in a few solemn words, which alarm the conscience." Ellicott: the command was "in some sense the first parable" — "the germ of an actual parable" (Matthew 13:47), the sea being "the troubled and evil world." Robertson: already disciples (John 1:35-42), they are "now called upon to leave their business."

Gill notes these had been John's disciples and had had "conversation" with Christ before, but "abode with him but for" a time; now they are called to constant following.

So the witnesses agree: Christ's call is brief, solemn, and transforming — "follow me" — and carries a promise that lifts their trade into its image: the catchers of fish shall become catchers of men. The men who had met him in Judaea are now summoned to leave all and attach themselves to him for the work of winning souls, the first parable of the kingdom enacted in their own calling (v.20).

So the witnesses read the call as brief, solemn, and transforming: 'follow me,' with a promise that lifts their trade into its image — catchers of fish become catchers of men. Ellicott's that it is the germ of a parable and Barnes's that it means to win souls together frame the summons to leave all for the work of the kingdom (v.20).

The men who had met him in Judaea are now summoned to leave all and attach themselves to him for the winning of souls (v.20).

The first parable of the kingdom is enacted in their own calling, fishers of fish made fishers of men.

So the call lifts their humble trade into the image of their apostolic work.
20:
"And they straightway left their nets, and followed him." A brief verse, and the witnesses read the prompt, total obedience of the first disciples.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers mark the immediacy. Barnes: "immediately — as all should do when the Lord Jesus calls them." Their nets "were the means of their living, perhaps all their property," so leaving them "immediately" gave "every evidence of sincerity." Clarke: "a change, as far as it respected secular things, every way to their disadvantage," which "the proud and profane may exult" over, but which proved their wholehearted devotion. Gill: "they gave up themselves to his service, and became his disciples."

The verse is short but weighty: the response matches the call. Where Christ says "follow," they follow at once, abandoning the nets that were their livelihood without delay or condition.

So the witnesses agree: the disciples' obedience is immediate and complete — leaving their nets, the very means of their living, the moment Christ calls. Their prompt forsaking of all is held up as the pattern of true discipleship: when the Lord summons, his own respond without hesitation, counting his service above their trade. The same readiness will mark the calling of the next pair (v.22).

So the witnesses read the disciples' obedience as immediate and complete: leaving their nets, their very livelihood, the moment Christ calls. Barnes's 'as all should do when the Lord calls' and Clarke's that it was every way to their worldly disadvantage together hold up their prompt forsaking as the pattern of true discipleship (v.22).

When the Lord summons, his own respond without hesitation, counting his service above their trade (v.22).

Their prompt forsaking of their nets is held up as the very pattern of true discipleship, leaving all at the word of Christ (Lk 5:11).

They leave the means of their living at once, giving every evidence of a sincere devotion.

So their immediate obedience stands as the model of forsaking all for Christ.

At the word of Christ they leave their nets without delay or condition.

Such ready obedience is the sincere mark of those whom the Lord effectually calls.
21:
"And going on from thence, he saw other two brethren, James the son of Zebedee, and John his brother... mending their nets; and he called them." The witnesses read the calling of the second pair of brothers.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the detail. Robertson: "these two brothers, James and John, were getting their nets ready for use"; the verb (katartizō) "means to adjust, to articulate, to mend if needed" (Galatians 6:1). Ellicott notes that, on the assumption that Luke's events preceded, the "mending" might be "the natural consequence of the breaking" there (Luke 5:6) — "an undesigned coincidence." Lightfoot adds a rabbinic namesake of James.

Gill: "he spied two other persons he was looking for, and had designed to call to the office of apostleship," again "brethren" and named.

So the witnesses agree: Christ, walking on, calls a second pair of fishermen — James and John, sons of Zebedee — found mending their nets, and summons them as he had Peter and Andrew. The deliberate choice of two more humble brothers, busy at their craft, continues the pattern: the Lord builds his apostolic band from working fishermen, called from their boats to follow him (v.22).

So the witnesses read the calling of the second pair: James and John, found mending their nets, summoned as Peter and Andrew were. The moderns' note on the verb 'mending' (perhaps the undesigned coincidence of Luke 5:6) shows the Lord building his apostolic band from working fishermen called from their boats (v.22).

The deliberate choice of two more humble brothers, busy at their craft, continues the pattern of the Lord's calling (v.22).

The Lord continues to build his band from working men, called from their boats to follow him (Mk 1:19).

So a second pair of brothers is summoned, as the first, from their nets to follow Christ.

The apostolic band grows by the same call, from the same lakeside trade.

From the boats of Galilee the Lord calls the preachers of his kingdom.

The second pair, like the first, are found at their craft and summoned to follow.
22:
"And they immediately left the ship and their father, and followed him." The witnesses read the second pair's obedience — even more costly than the first.

The grammatical-historical and Wesleyan-Arminian readers mark what was forsaken. Gill: "more is expressed here than before, for they not only left their nets, but their ship too, which was of much more value; nay, even their father also, with the hired servants." Barnes: "this showed how willing they were to follow Jesus. They left their father... If necessary, we should leave father, and mother, and every friend" (Luke 14:26). Clarke and Ellicott note Mark's "with the hired servants" (Mark 1:20), suggesting the sons of Zebedee were of "better means and higher social standing."

The Reformed voice reads the inward power. Calvin: "the power of Christ's voice" — "not that his voice alone makes so powerful an impression... but those whom the Lord is pleased to draw to himself are inwardly addressed by his Spirit, that they may obey his voice."

So the witnesses agree: James and John leave even more than Peter and Andrew — boat, livelihood, and father — at the call of Christ, the Spirit inwardly drawing them to obey. Their immediate forsaking of family and trade alike completes the picture of wholehearted discipleship, and the four fishermen become the first of the band Christ will lead through Galilee (v.23).

So the witnesses read the second pair's obedience as even more costly: they leave boat, trade, and father at Christ's call. Calvin's that the power of his voice rests on the inward drawing of his Spirit, and Barnes's that we must leave even family if need be (Lk 14:26), together complete the picture of wholehearted discipleship (v.23).

Their immediate forsaking of family and trade alike completes the picture of wholehearted discipleship, and the four become the first of the band (v.23).

The four fishermen become the first of the company Christ will lead through Galilee.

The power of his voice rests on the inward drawing of the Spirit, who makes the called willing to obey.
23:
"And Jesus went about all Galilee, teaching in their synagogues, and preaching the gospel of the kingdom, and healing all manner of sickness." The witnesses read the threefold summary of Christ's Galilean ministry.

The Latin and Eastern fathers read the order and the array. Pseudo-Chrysostom: "kings, when about to go to war, first gather an army" — so the Lord, about to war against the Devil, "first collects" his soldiers. Remigius draws "an example for doctors" — they should "not be inactive" ("Jesus went about"), nor partial. Rabanus and Jerome expound the sicknesses healed.

The grammatical-historical and Puritan readers fix the work. Robertson: "the first of the three tours of Galilee," with "the four fishermen whom he had just called." Ellicott: "the gospel of the kingdom... tells of a vast amount of unrecorded teaching." Henry: "what an industrious preacher Christ was — he went about... teaching in their synagogues." Lightfoot and Clarke supply the history of the synagogues.

The Reformed voice reads the union of word and work. Calvin: though Christ "was constantly employed in performing almost innumerable miracles," the general summaries are not strange. So the witnesses agree: Christ traverses all Galilee in a ministry of teaching, preaching the good news of the kingdom, and healing every disease — word and miracle joined, the gospel proclaimed and confirmed — drawing the multitudes whose gathering the next verses describe (v.24).

So the witnesses read the threefold summary — teaching, preaching the gospel of the kingdom, healing — as word and miracle joined. The fathers' image of the king gathering his army before war, and the moderns' that this is the first of three Galilean tours, together frame a ministry that proclaims the good news and confirms it by power (v.24).

Word and miracle joined, the gospel proclaimed and confirmed, draw the multitudes whose gathering the next verses describe (v.24).

The gospel of the kingdom is proclaimed in word and confirmed in deed.

So the ministry of teaching, preaching, and healing draws all Galilee after him.

So word and miracle together open the great Galilean ministry.
24:
"And his fame went throughout all Syria: and they brought unto him all sick people... and he healed them." The witnesses read the spreading report and the breadth of Christ's healing.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scope. Robertson: "rumour carries things almost like the wireless" — "the Gentiles all over Syria heard," and "Jesus had a moving hospital." Barnes defines Syria as "the general name for the country... between the Euphrates and the Mediterranean." Clarke lists the maladies — the sick, those "taken with divers diseases and torments" (racked in every joint), "possessed with devils," lunatics, and palsied. Ellicott notes "Syria" is "used popularly."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Wesley: "demoniacs — men possessed with devils; and lunatics, and paralytics — men ill of the palsy, whose cases were of all others most deplorable and most helpless."

So the witnesses agree: the report of Christ spreads far beyond Galilee, even to Gentile Syria, and they bring him every kind of sufferer — the diseased, the tormented, the demon-possessed, the epileptic, the paralyzed — and he heals them all. The unrestricted compassion and power of the healing, reaching the most hopeless cases, both confirm his preaching and draw the vast multitudes that follow (v.25).

So the witnesses read the spreading fame and the breadth of the healing: the report reaches even Gentile Syria, and every kind of sufferer — diseased, demon-possessed, lunatic, palsied — is brought and healed. Robertson's 'moving hospital' and Wesley's on the most hopeless cases together show the unrestricted compassion that draws the multitudes (v.25).

The unrestricted compassion and power of the healing, reaching the most hopeless cases, both confirm his preaching and draw the vast multitudes (v.25).

The most deplorable and helpless cases of all are brought to him and healed.

So the unrestricted power and compassion of the healing confirm the gospel he proclaims.

The fame of such healing draws the multitudes from every region.

His healing power reaches even the most hopeless and helpless cases.

From all the surrounding regions the afflicted are carried to him, and not one is turned away unhealed.
25:
"And there followed him great multitudes of people from Galilee, and from Decapolis, and from Jerusalem, and from Judaea, and from beyond Jordan." The witnesses read the vast crowds drawn from every quarter — the setting for the Sermon on the Mount.

The grammatical-historical readers fix the scope. Robertson: "note the plural, not just one crowd, but crowds and crowds," from "all parts of Palestine including Decapolis" — "no political campaign was equal to this outpouring of the people." Barnes and Ellicott explain Decapolis, "the ten cities" east and south-east of the sea of Galilee, a district formed by the Romans. Wesley: "a tract of land on the east side of the sea of Galilee, in which were ten cities."

The Wesleyan-Arminian Clarke notes the verse "is immediately connected with the fifth chapter, and should not be separated from it" — the multitudes are the audience of the Sermon; "great multitudes," even by Jewish reckoning, were "one proof of the days of the Messiah." Gill marks the mixed motives — "some out of good will, others out of ill will... some to see his miracles, others to hear his doctrine."

So the witnesses agree: from Galilee, Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judaea, and beyond Jordan the multitudes pour after Christ — an outpouring unmatched by any cause — drawn by his preaching and his healing. The gathering of these vast and varied crowds closes the chapter and sets the stage for the great discourse that follows, the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1).

So the witnesses read the vast crowds from every quarter — Galilee, Decapolis, Jerusalem, Judaea, beyond Jordan — as an outpouring unmatched by any cause. Clarke's that the verse joins to chapter 5 makes these the audience of the Sermon on the Mount, the gathering of the multitudes setting the stage for the great discourse (Matthew 5:1).

The gathering of these vast and varied crowds closes the chapter and sets the stage for the Sermon on the Mount (Matthew 5:1).

From every quarter the multitudes pour after Christ, an outpouring unmatched by any earthly cause.
